attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a four-year history of progressive breathlessness and dry cough. He reports reduced exercise tolerance, having to slow down whilst doing the grocery shopping. His breathlessness is impacting daily activities. He has smoked 20 cigarettes daily for 40 years and continues to smoke. He has a medical history of seasonal asthma which is well-controlled with as-needed salbutamol. He does not take any regular medications. He has no history of concerning occupational exposure.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Chest auscultation reveals a mildly prolonged expiratory phase but is otherwise clear.

Pulmonary function tests demonstrate:

  • FEV1: 59% of predicted

  • FVC: 80% of predicted

  • FEV1/FVC Ratio: 67% of predicted

There is no significant reversibility with short-acting bronchodilators. Chest X-ray is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management in addition to smoking cessation?
options: Long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) plus long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) dual therapy
Long-acting beta-agonist (LABA), long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) and inhaled corticosteroid triple therapy
Low-dose inhaled corticosteroid plus as-needed short-acting beta-agonist (SABA)
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) including progressive dyspnoea, reduced exercise tolerance, and significant smoking history. The diagnosis is confirmed by pulmonary function tests showing fixed airflow obstruction with FEV1/FVC ratio of 67% (which is less than 70%), FEV1 at 59% of predicted, and minimal bronchodilator reversibility.

According to the Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) guidelines, patients are classified into Groups A, B, or E based on symptom burden and exacerbation history. To differentiate these groups: first, assess exacerbation history - if the patient has had two or more moderate exacerbations or one or more hospitalisations for COPD in the past year, they are Group E. If not, then assess symptom burden using the modified Medical Research Council (mMRC) dyspnoea scale or COPD Assessment Test (CAT) score. Patients with mMRC 0-1 or CAT <10 are Group A (low symptoms), while those with mMRC ≥2 or CAT ≥10 are Group B (high symptoms).

This patient's breathlessness impacting daily activities and slowing down during grocery shopping corresponds to an mMRC score of 2 (walks slower than people of the same age on level ground because of breathlessness). With an mMRC score ≥2 and no history of exacerbations, this patient falls into GOLD Group B.

Long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) plus long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) dual therapy is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological management for GOLD Group B patients. The GOLD guidelines specifically recommend dual bronchodilation as initial therapy for Group B patients, as this combination provides superior symptom control and improvement in lung function compared to monotherapy. These medications work through different mechanisms to relax airway smooth muscle, reduce hyperinflation, and improve exercise capacity.

Inhaled corticosteroid monotherapy is not recommended as initial therapy for COPD. Current guidelines reserve inhaled corticosteroids for patients with features suggesting steroid responsiveness, such as blood eosinophilia (≥300 cells/μL) or concurrent asthma with frequent exacerbations. While this patient has a history of asthma, his current presentation is consistent with COPD without exacerbations, making ICS monotherapy inappropriate.

Long-acting beta-agonist (LABA), long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) and inhaled corticosteroid triple therapy is reserved for GOLD Group E patients who have frequent exacerbations (≥2 moderate or ≥1 leading to hospitalisation in the past year) despite dual bronchodilator therapy, particularly those with blood eosinophilia. This patient has no history of exacerbations, making triple therapy excessive as initial treatment.

Low-dose inhaled corticosteroid plus as-needed short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) is an approach more commonly used in asthma management rather than COPD. For GOLD Group A patients (mMRC 0-1, 0-1 moderate exacerbations), a long-acting bronchodilator (either LABA or LAMA) would be appropriate as initial therapy, but this patient's symptom burden places him in Group B, requiring dual bronchodilation.

Long-term macrolide antibiotics may be considered in patients with severe COPD who continue to have frequent exacerbations despite optimal inhaler therapy. They are used for their anti-inflammatory properties rather than antimicrobial effects. However, they are not first-line therapy and carry risks including antimicrobial resistance, QT prolongation, and hearing loss. This patient has no history of frequent exacerbations that would warrant this approach.

The cornerstone of COPD management remains smoking cessation, which is the only intervention proven to slow disease progression. All patients should be offered comprehensive smoking cessation support including behavioural therapy and pharmacological aids if appropriate.
learningPoints: Smoking cessation is the most crucial intervention to improve long-term outcomes in COPD.
Long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) plus long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) combination therapy is first-line pharmacological management for GOLD Group B COPD patients.
COPD patients are classified into Groups A, B, or E based on symptom burden (mMRC or CAT score) and exacerbation history, guiding initial pharmacological therapy.
references: Yang IA, George J, McDonald CF, McDonald V, Ordman R, Goodwin A, Smith B, McNamara R, Zwar N, Dabscheck E. The COPD-X Plan: Australian and New Zealand Guidelines for the management of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease 2023. Version 2.73, December 2023.
Hopkinson NS, Molyneux A, Pink J, Harrisingh MC. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: diagnosis and management: summary of updated NICE guidance. Bmj. 2019 Jul 29;366.
Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease [published 2020 Dec; amended 2025 Feb]. In: Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited; accessed {26 May 2025}. https://www.tg.org.au

Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease. Global Strategy for the Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: 2025 Report [Internet]. Fontana (WI): Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease; 2024 [cited 2025 Jun 26]. Available from: https://goldcopd.org/2025-gold-report/
